text: BANNANEL. See BANANAL.
BANNASOU, a town of Upper Guinea, in the kingdom of Akim, near the r. bank of the Busumpra, and 50 m. SE of Komassie.
BANNATO. See BANAT.
BANNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, and in the cant. and 4 m. from Sancerre. Pop. 644.
BANNBRIDGE. See BANBRIDGE.
BANNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, and cant. of Les Vares. Pop. 1,755.
BANNEGON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cher, and cant. of Charenton, 10 m. NE of St. Amand. Pop. 757.
BANNIACHUNG, or Baniachung, a town of India beyond the Ganges, in the prov. of Tipperah, 10 m. E of Azmerigunge, and 45 m. SW of Silhet.
BANNINGHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. NE of Aylsham. Area 770 acres. Pop. 329.
BANNIO, or Banio, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 11 m. SW of Domo d'Ossolo, and 37 m. NNE of Ivrea, on the r. bank of the Anzasca.

BANNISTER, a river of Virginia, U.S., which takes its rise in the W of Pittsylvania co., and running NE and SE, unites with the Dan in the E of Halifax co., 10 m. above the junction of that river with the Roanoke.—Also a town of Virginia, cap. of Halifax co., to the S of the river of the same name.
BANNOA, a town of Siam, on a tributary of the Bang-pa-Lung river, 145 m. NE of Bangkok.

BANNOCKBURN, a village in the p. of St. Ninian's, in Stirlingshire, on the road from Stirling to Falkirk, 2 m. SE of Stirling, and 9 m. NW of Falkirk. It is intersected by the Bannock, which divides it into two parts, known as Upper and Lower Bannockburn. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the woollen manufacture, especially in that of tartans. The Bannock rises in Loch Coulter, on the S side of the Gillies hill, and flows in a winding course, between steep and rugged banks, eastwards to Milton, where it turns towards the NE, winding in that direction, through a deep and rugged valley, to the village of B., and, after a course of a few miles, falls into the Forth at a place called Manor opposite Black Grange. The famous and decisive battle of BAN Bannockburn was fought in the neighbourhood of this v., on Monday, June 24th, 1314.

BANNOW, a parish containing the almost extinct traces of an ancient town and a borough of the same name, in co. Wexford. It also contains the village of Carrick. Area 6,551 acres. Pop. 2,378.—The extinct old town and modern hamlet of B. is 16 m. SW of Wexford. There is no inhabitant or house on any part of what was the old town of B. Part of the site of this ancient town has been covered with sand drifted from the sea, which, in some places, covers the original surface to the depth of many feet.
BANNOW, or Banow, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 11 m. SE of Hradisch, on a tributary of the Morava. It is celebrated for its mineral waters. Pop. 950.

BANNOW BAY, an estuary or marine inlet, called sometimes Bannow harbour, and sometimes Bannow river, on the S coast of co. Wexford. It enters by a narrow channel from the head of Ballyteigue bay, and ascends 31 m. NE.
BANOLAS, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, 8 m. NW of Gerona, and 60 m. NNE of Barcelona. It has considerable trade in linen. Pop. 3,000.

BANON, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, and arrond. of Forcalquier. The cant. comprises 11 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 3,171. The village is 10 m. NW of Forcalquier. Pop. 1,337.

BANOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Palencia, 3 m. N of the junction of the Pisuerga and Sequillo, and 5 m. S of Palencia.—Also a village in the prov. and 42 m. S of Salamanca, and 5 m. SW of Bejar. In the vicinity are the thermal springs which were known to the Romans, and the baths from which the vil. derives its name. Pop. 1,700.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, and cant. of St. Sever, which affords good white wines. Pop. 432.— Also a town of Ecuador, in the prov. and 80 m. S of Quito, near the S bank of the Pastaca, and at the NE base of the mountain of Tunguragua. Its baths are much frequented.
BANOS-DE-EBRO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. W of Logrono, on the r. bank of the Ebro.
BANOS (Torre DE), a town of Spain, in Andalusia, 8 m. SW of Marbella, and 48 m. SW of Malaga, on the Mediterranean.
BANOWICZ. See BAN.
BANSBAREA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the E bank of the Hoogly, and nearly 4 m. NNW of the town of that name.
BANSDA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, 47 m. SE of Surat.
BANSEY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, in the dist. and 35 m. NW of Goruckpoor, on the l. bank of the Rapty.
BANSHA, or Banshaw, a village in the p. of Templeneiry, co. Tipperary, 5 m. SSE of Tipperary. Pop. 378.
BANSKOW, a town of the duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwern, on the l. bank of the Elde, 5 m. S of the entrance of that river into Schwern lake, and 8 m. SSE of the town of that name.
BANSTEAD, a parish of England, in Surrey, 3 m. SE of Ervell. Area 5,840 acres. Pop. 1,768.

BANSWARRA, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of a small principality of the same name, in the prov. of Gujerat, and dist. of Bagur, 130 m. ENE of Ahmedabad, and 90 SE of Odipur, in N lat. 23° 31', and E long. 74° 37'. The town is one of the best description in the prov. It possesses some fine temples, a good bazaar, and contains within the circuit of its walls gardens of considerable extent. In the vicinity, situated on a small lake or pool, are several temples now in ruins; and rising beyond is a woody eminence surmounted by a castle, the former residence of the rawul or prince of B. The territory is divided into 7 dist., viz., Chownden, Ganthee, Loharia, Mhye-Outar, Ootaur, Pithok, and Sirhoh.
BANTAL, a town of the island of Sumatra, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the W coast, at the mouth of a small river which flows into the Indian ocean opposite Poggy island.
BANTALLAN, an islet of the Philippine group, in the Asiatic archipelago, at the northern entrance of the channel which separates the islands of Negros and Zebu, in N lat. 11°, and E long. 123° 25'.
BANTA LUNG, a town of Siam, on a tributary of the Bang-pa-Kung river, 140 m. NE of Bangkok.

BANTAM, a residency or kingdom on the NW coast of Java. The cap. of the same name, in S lat. 6° 2', E long. 106° 9' [S lat. 6° 20', E long. 105° 26', Voy. of Potomac], 61 in. W of Batavia, was formerly the principal resort of European vessels visiting that island. This kingdom, founded about the close of the 15th cent., by a Mahommedan chief, son of the sultan of Cheribon, was anciently of great extent, being computed to be between 600 and 700 m. in circuit. Its boundaries were on the S the Indian ocean; on the W and NW the straits of Sunda, separating it from Sumatra; towards the interior it was separated from Jacatra by a chain of mountains. It has now declined from its ancient consequence, and its territories have been reduced by encroachments, but its area is estimated at 3,428 sq. m. The cultivation of rice, and the breeding and rearing of cattle, form the principal branches of industry in this district. Coarse cotton cloths are manufactured. The pop., according to an estimate made in 1815, was reduced to 231,604; but it receives constant accessions from the desertion of the Madurans, slaves, bankrupt Chinese, and even assassins, from the Dutch settlements, who take refuge here in places where the hand of justice cannot follow them. -The city of Bantam was once a rich and flourishing place, but it is now a miserable village. Fort Spielwick, belonging to the Dutch, and another in which the sultan resided, no longer exist; and most of the dwellings of Europeans here have been abandoned. The town stands about a mile from the sea, and 15 m. E of the Sunda strait, in a low marshy situation. It is extremely unhealthy, insomuch, that of 50 persons who were sent in 1804 to witness the installation of a new sultan, not above 8 or 10 were alive in a fortnight. The town of Ceram or Serang, about 7 m. inland, is now the residence of the European officers of the district.—The Dutch first arrived here in 1595; and having assisted the king against the Portuguese, obtained his permission to build a fort. They were thus enabled to control the whole trade in pepper—the principal product of the country,—and of which it exported 3,000,000 lbs. The English also established a factory here in 1602 or 1603, and even made it the cap. of their Eastern possessions; but the agent and most of the settlers being afterwards treacherously murdered, the Dutch were left without rivals; and having established residents in various places, advanced gradually until they not only rendered it tributary to themselves, but bound the sovereign by engagements to dispose of none of it to other nations. In 1683 they erected Fort Spielwick, which commanded the whole town; and in the course of the succeeding cent. the natives fell completely under their yoke. Although the king preserved the appearance of power, his sovereignty was at an end. During the late war, when the Dutch were defeated at Batavia, and that city was occupied by the British forces, B. surrendered without resistance in 1811. It was restored to the Dutch in 1814. It is not likely that B. can ever regain its former con- BAN sequence; for, independent of its trade being transferred to other channels, the bay and harbour are so much choked up with accessions of earth washed down from the mountains, and the growth of coral shoals, as to have become inaccessible to ships of burden. The tides rise here between 5 and 6 ft. The bay, which is extensive, contains several islands, the longest of which is Pulo Panjang. Good anchorage in 14 to 16 fath. will be found at Pangoriang, 4 m. E of Point St. Nicholas. Wood and water can be readily procured here; and a great variety of fruits and vegetables. The coast from B. to Batavia is flat and low, but ever changing, and ever beautiful; and the sea is studded with little evergreen islands of great beauty and every shape. - Stavorinus. - Raffles. - Crawfurd. - Voyage of the Potomac.
BANTAM (CAPE), a promontory on the E coast of Cochin-China, forming the E side of Quiquite harbour, in N lat. 15° 27', and E long. 108° 45'.
BANTANGAN (CAPE), a headland of the E coast of Cochin-China, in N lat. 15° 15', and E long. 108° 50'.
BANTEER, a village in the p. of Clonmeen, co. Cork. Pop. 137.
BANTJAR, a town of the island of Java, on the N coast, in the residency of Rembang. It possesses a port and good building docks.
BANTON, an islet of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, to the E of Mindora, in N lat. 12° 55'.
BANTOUZEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Marcoing, near the r. bank of the Schelde, 9 m. S of Cambray. It has tile-works and manufactories of pottery. Pop. 920.
BANTRANG, a town of Siam, on the r. bank of the Menam, 22 m. NNE of Bangkok.

BANTRY, a spacious bay in co. Cork. It penetrates the country ENE; and is separated from Dunmanus bay, on the r., by a peninsula of <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in mean breadth, -and from the Kenmare river or estuary, on the l., by a peninsula of about 7 m. in mean breadth. It enters between Sheep-head on the r., and Blackhall-head, or the most southerly termination of the Berehaven mountains, on the l.; and it is 8 m. wide at the entrance. Its length is 20 m.; and its interior breadth varies between 23 m. and 8 m., and in general is upwards of 5 m. The principal islands are Bere and Whiddy, the former covering Berehaven, and the latter lying off the town of Bantry; and on the E side of Whiddy are four isles, called Chapel, Horse, Hog, and Rabbit islands. The scenery of Bantry bay is thought by some to be unexcelled by any in the kingdom.

BANTRY, a post, market, and sea-port town, in the p. of Kilmacomogue, co. Cork, at the head of a large cove at the SE extremity of Bantry bay, 10 m. WNW of Skibbereen, and 171 m. SW of Dublin. The original town was called Ballygobbin; and occupied the site of the present town. In 1830, the harbour of B.—which technically extends, we believe, over all B. bay, and must not be identified exclusively with the town—had 8 decked vessels, aggregately carrying 170 tons and 46 men; 5 half-decked vessels, carrying 68 tons and 24 men; 59 open sail-boats, carrying 294 men; and 641 row-boats, carrying 3,341 men. Pop. in 1831, 4,275; in 1841, 4,082. Houses 613.
BANTWAH, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, 30 m. W of Junaghur.
BANTZ, or BANZ, a village and seignory of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Mayne, 3 m. from Lichtenfels. It has a fine ducal castle, which was formerly a Benedictine monastery. Pop. 90.
BANTZENHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, and cant. of Habsheim. Pop. 1,133.
BANUB, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Garbieh, on the l. bank of the Tabarieh canal, 8 m. | NNE of Mahalet. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Onuphis.
BANUELOS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 19 m. NE of Burgos, and 4 m. S of Briviesca, near the r. bank of the Pecezorias.
BANVILLE, a village of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Doudeville, 13 m. NNE of Yvetot, and 27 m. NNW of Rouen. It has a calico printing-mill.
BANVOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, and cant. of Missei. Pop. 1,054.
BANWELL, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. NNW of Axbridge. Area 4,970 acres. Pop. 1,168.
BANYA, a Hungarian word signifying 'a mine,' and forming a frequent prefix in Hungarian topography.

BANYA-FELSO, or Ungrisch-Neustadt, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Szathmar, 100 m. E of Debrecin, and 3 m. E of Nagy-Banya. It has a Catholic church; and mines of gold, silver, copper, lead and iron, are wrought in the vicinity. Copper and lead founderies, forges and manufactories of pottery, exist here. Pop. 4,700.
BANYA-KIS, a town of Transylvania, in the circle and 10 m. NW of Thorda, and 65 m. NNW of Hermanstadt. It has mines of argentiferous lead.

BANYA-NAGY, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Szathmar, on an affluent of the Szamos, 11 m. E of Erdod-Szada, and 95 m. E of Debrecin. It is the cap. of the four great mining districts of Hungary, and has a mint, 4 churches belonging to different sects, a Catholic gymnasium, and a normal school. The mines in the environs produce a considerable annual amount of gold and silver, besides copper, lead, &c. The principal silver smelting-furnaces are at Fernezely, and the copper founderies at Lapos- Banya. Pop. 4,928.

BANYULS-SUR-MER, or BANYULS-DE-MARENDE, a town of France, in the Pyrenees-Orientales, and cant. of Argele, on the Mediterranean. It has a small port, the shipping of which in 1837 amounted to 1,283 tons. The adjacent shores abound with sardines and anchovies, the salting of which forms an important branch of the industry of the locality; and the environs of B. afford excellent descriptions of wine. Pop. 1,608.

BANYU WANGI, a district and town of the island of Java, situated at its western extremity, on the straits of Bally. The district comprises a superficial area of 1,274 sq. m.; and in 1815 contained a pop. of 8,873, of whom 319 were Chinese. It is generally well cultivated and salubrious. Its most remarkable physical feature is the lofty volcanic mountain of Gunong-mar-api, which affords sulphur of the purest description. The town, which is 550 m. ESE of Batavia, in S lat. 8° 15', and E long. 114° 20', is populous, and forms an important military post.

BAOL, an independent state of W Africa, in Senegambia, bounded on the N by the kingdom of Kayor, from which it is partially separated by the river Somon, on the E by the Jaloff territory, on the S by the kingdom of Sin, and on the W by the Atlantic ocean.
BAOM ISLAND. See APAE.
BAONS (Les), a town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, and cant. of Yerville, 2 m. NNE of Yvetot. Pop. 550.

BAOUSK, or Bausk, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Courland and dist. of Mitau, at the confluence of the Memel and Alass, 40 m. above the entrance of the Aa into the gulf of Livonia, and 20 m. SE of Mitau. It is ill-built, but possesses considerable commerce in grain, and has extensive breweries. Pop. 936.

BAPAUME, a canton, commune, and town of CONTRACTOR OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF BAR France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and arrond. of Arras. The cant. comprises 22 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,741. The town is 15 m. SSE of Arras. It is a place of considerable strength, and is an artillery station. It is tolerably regularly built, and contains a parish church, a handsome hospital, and a communal college. It has also extensive manufactories of cambric, calicoes, thread, sugar from beet-root, &c., and spinning-mills. Its cambrics form its principal article of commerce. It has 3 annual fairs, the trade of which consists in jewellery, hardware, &c. This town was taken by Louis in 1641, and in 1659 was ceded to Louis by the treaty of the Pyrenees. The surrounding district is extremely arid; and a single fountain affords the only supply of water which the town possesses. Pop. 3,195.

BAPAUME-LES-ROUEN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, and cant. of Maromme, on the Cailly, near Canteleu. It has cotton spinning, and thread mills, calico-manufactories, and paper and calico-printing mills. Pop. 565.
BAPAW, a village of Beluchistan, in the Gundava pass, 50 m. S of Kelat, at an alt. of 5,000 ft. above sea-level.
BAPCHILD, a parish in Kent, 1 m. SE of Sittingbourne. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 328.
BAQUERO, a town of La Plata, on a river of the same name, a tributary of the Lavayen, 65 m. WSW of the confluence of the latter with the Rio Grande, and 15 m. N of Salta.

BAR, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Podolia, 40 m. N of Mohilev, on the river Row. Pop. 3,000. It is fortified; and is celebrated for the confederation of the Polish nobles, Pulaski, Potocki, and Krasinski, against the Russian government, which was entered into here on the 29th of Feb. 1768.—Also a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, on the r. bank of the Ganges, 20 m. NE of Bahar. Area of district 166 sq. m. Pop. 96,950.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. and the cant. of Corréze, 6 m. N of Tulle. Pop. 1,228.—Also a river of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, rising near Butancy, and joining the Meuse on the l. bank, near Donchery, after a course of 45 m.
BAR (GREAT), a chapelry in the parish of Aldridge, Staffordshire. Area 4,960 acres. Pop. 1,087.
BAR (LE), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. and 6 m. NE of Grasse. Pop. of com. 1,504; of cant. comprising 10 com., 6,823 in 1831.

BAR-SUR-AUBE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of l'Aube. -The arrond. has an area of 105,000 hectares, and comprises 4 cantons, viz., Bar-sur-Aube, Brienne-le- Chateau, Soulaines, and Vendeuvre. Pop. in 1836, 41,230; in 1846, 42,634.—The cant. comprises 23 com.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Aube, 30 m. E of Troyes, and 125 m. ESE of Paris, had a pop. in 1789 of 3,848; in 1836, of 3,940; and in 1846, of 4,133. It is an ancient town, and at one time possessed extensive trade and commerce, merchants from all quarters of Europe resorting to its four annual fairs. At present its trade chiefly consists in wines, brandies, coarse cotton and woollen fabrics, and grain.—A severe conflict took place here with the allied forces on the 25th of May, 1814, which is, as usual, chronicled in the French gazetteers as having resulted in a victory to the French arms, though it did not stay the march of the enemy upon Paris.

BAR-SUR-ORNAIN, or BAR-LE-Duc, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La.Meuse.—The arrond, has an area of 146,973 hectares; and comprises 8 cantons, viz., Ancerville, Bar-le-Duc, Ligny, Montiers-sur-Saux, Revigny, Triaucourt, Vaubécourt, and Vavincourt. Pop. in 1836, 80,952; in 1846, 82,109.—The cant. comprises 8 com.-The com. and town are situated on the Ornain, a tributary of the Marne, 152 m. E of Paris, in N lat. 48° 46′ 8″, E long. 5° 9′; at an alt. of 185 metres, or 607 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1789, 10,322; in 1836, 12,383; in 1846, 12,230. The town rises on the side of a hill, and is divided into an upper and a lower town; the lower and the more extensive is the seat of its trade; the upper was at one period almost exclusively inhabited by the noblesse, who fled from it in a body at the breaking-forth of the Revolution. Previous to that event, the religious edifices and establishments in the town were numerous; but they have been much reduced both in number and in magnificence in later years. The principal manufactures are leather, brandy, oil, hosiery, gloves, cotton-yarn, and sweetmeats. A good deal of traffic is carried on by means of the river, which is navigable. - This town, anciently the cap. of the duchy of the same name, originated in a castle which was founded here in the 10th cent., by Frederick, the brother-in-law of Hugh Capet, to defend Lorraine from the people of Champagne.—The environs abound in iron-ore, and in mineral springs.

BAR-SUR-SEINE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aube. -The arrond. has an area of 128,000 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 52,117; in 1846, 52,029. It comprises the 5 cantons of Bar-sur-Seine, Chaource, Essoyes, Mussy-sur-Seine, and Les Riceys.-The cant. comprises 22 com.—The com. and town are situated on the l. bank of the Seine, below the junction of the Ource with that river, 18 m. SE of Troyes. Pop. in 1789, 2,522; in 1836, 2,350. It is a place of little trade save as an entrepot for wine. It suffered frequently during the wars between the French and English in the 14th and 15th cent.
BAR (BIR-EL-), or BEREMBA, a village of Upper Egypt, on the r. bank of the Nile, 10 m. S of Ghenneh.

BARA, a river of Afghanistan, rising in the territory of the Afreedies, and flowing into the Cabul after a course of about 60 m.—Also a town of Venezuela, near the mouth of a small river flowing into the Caribbean sea, in W long. 69°.—Also the W head-stream of the Assinee river, in W Africa.

BARABA, a steppe or extensive plain on the western verge of Siberia, situated between the rivers Irtish and Obi. This steppe, exceeding 600 versts in length from N to S, and 400 in breadth from E to W, is a continued flat, diversified only by forests of birch-wood, and by lakes both salt and fresh. The soil and the climate are extremely favourable to agriculture: some of the lands are indeed low and marshy, but in general they are either covered with fine herbage, or with luxuriant crops of grain. The southern part of the plain, towards the Irtish and the mountains of Altai, is the driest, but it is likewise the most barren and the least adorned with wood. Deep forests spread over the N, and overshadow the lower banks of the Obi. The native pop. of this steppe are called by the Russians Barabinzes, or Barabinzians; and, from the diversity of their features, appear to be descended from several different tribes. They had been successively conquered by the Kirghises and Sungarians; and when Siberia was subdued by the Russians, could relate no particulars whatever of their history. The greater part of them appear from their physiognomy to be of Tartar origin; the long, half-opened eyes of others of them, indicate their descent from the Mongols; while the Kalmuk countenances of a third part of them bespeak their affinity with the Sungarians, their former conquerors. Every village in the B. is under the direction of a chief, and every district is governed by a sort of prince, called a | TOLOTATLAN. BAR yaouta. The Barabinzians were conquered by the Russians in 1595; but even after that time were often harassed by the incursions of the Kirghises and Sungarians, the latter of whom compelled them to pay an annual tribute. Since the Siberian frontier-line was properly defined, they have enjoyed complete tranquillity under the protection of the Russians. They number about 5,000 bows. Most of them have abjured Paganism, and embraced the religion of Mahomet.—Tooke's View of the Russian empire.

BARABUTTY, or BARABATI, a fortress of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, situated on the Mahanuddy, about a mile from Cuttack. It is of a rectangular form, 2,180 ft. in length, and 1,800 ft. in breadth; and is surrounded by a double stone wall, and a strongly embanked ditch, 20 ft. in depth, and in some parts 220 ft. in breadth. In the centre is a massive square cavalier, surmounted by a flag-staff. This fortress, in the general character of its architecture, bears evidence of Hindu origin, and is supposed to have been erected in the 14th cent. Until its capture by the British in 1809, it was considered impregnable. Of the famous palace of Raja Mukund Deo, described by Abul Fazul as existing here, no vestiges are now to be discovered.

BARACOA, a town and harbour in the island of Cuba, near the SE extremity, 100 m. ENE of Santiago-de-Cuba, in N lat. 20° 17', and W long. 74° 25'. It has a good harbour, and is an important pilot-station for the Bahama or Old channel.
BARADERO, a town of La Plata, at the confluence of the Arecive with a bifurcation of the Parana, 100 m. NW of Buenos Ayres.
BARADRI, a village in Beluchistan, 68 m. NE of Kelat, near the entrance to the Bolan pass.
BARAGIANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, district of Potenza. Pop. 1,650.
BARAHAT, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of the rajah of Gurwal, on the N bank of the Ganges, in N lat. 30° 45′, 48 m. NNW of Serinagur.
BARAHONA, or BARAONA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 36 m. SSW of Soria.
BARAITCHE, a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, on the N side of the Goggra river. The town is 64 m. N of Lucknow.
BARAM, a mountain-ridge of Upper Egypt, 24 m. E of Assuan, and near the N frontier of Nubia, famous for its copper-mines.
BARAM, or BARRAM POINT, a promontory of the island of Borneo, on the NW coast, in N lat. 4° 25', E long. 113° 30′.
BARAMOUSA, a village of Lower Egypt, in the Natron valley, 65 m. WNW of Cairo. There is a Greek monastery here.
BARAMUN, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. and 5 m. NE of Mansurah, and 32 m. SW of Damietta, on the r. bank of the Damietta branch of the Nile.
BARAMUTTI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, 50 m. ESE of Poonah.
BARANCA, or BARRANCA, a river of Peru, in the prov. of Lima, which takes its rise in the Andes, and running in a WSW direction, falls into the Pacific in 10° 59' S, 90 m. NW of Lima.

BARANCA (LA), a town of Ecuador, in the dep. of Maynas, on the N bank of the Maranon, 60 m. ESE of St. Borja, and 80 WNW of La Laguna.—Also a town of New Grenada, in the prov. and 55 m. ESE of Carthagena, on the l. bank of the Magdalena. It has a good port, and considerable commerce.
BARANELLO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, and in the district and 5 m. SW of Campo- Basso. Pop. 2,663.
BARANIA. See GRANDE DI SANTIAGO RIO, OF
BARAN-LUKH, a town of Beluchistan, in the prov. of Jhalawan, 75 m. S of Khozdar.

BARANOFF, or Baranov (Cape), a promontory of Siberia, on the N coast of the country of the Tchuktchi, in N lat. 69° 45′, E long. 163° 50′. It is of basaltic formation, and contains numerous fossil remains.
BARANOH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Rajpootana, on the l. bank of the Chumbul, 40 m. NW of Kota.
BARANOVKA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, near the l. bank of the Slutch, 60 W of Jitomir.

BARANOW, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 45 m. NE of Tarnow, near the r. bank of the Vistula, at the confluence of the Ryska. It has a strong castle, built by king Stephen Bathory, and was the scene of the defeat of the Poles by Charles Gustavus, in 1656. Pop. 1,000.—Also a town of Prussia, in the regency of Posen, and circle of Schildberg, 1 m. SSE of Kempen, and 45 ENE of Breslau. It has two Catholic churches, and in 1837 contained a pop. of 845.—Also a town in Poland, 30 m. NW of Lublin, on the l. bank of the Wieprz, 12 m. above its confluence with the Vistula. Pop. 1,300.—Also a village in the gov. of Mazovia, 20 m. from Sochaczew. Pop. 450.
BARANOWO, a town of Poland, in the gov. of | Plock, 18 m. from Przasnysz. Pop. 560.
BARANQUILLA, or BARRANQUILLA, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. and 65 m. NE of Carthagena, on the l. side of the embouchure of the Magdalena. It has a good port, and extensive commerce.

BARANTCHINSK, a mining village of Russia, in the gov. of Perm, in the district and 69 m. SSW of Verkhofourie, on the Targil, and near the E base of the Ural mountains. It has blast and fining furnaces. The village and works belong to the Crown, and are under the joint administration of the mining government of Bogoslovsk and Blagodat.

BARANYA, a comitat or province of Hungary, bounded on the N by the circles of Sumeg or Somogy, and Tolna; on the E by the Danube; on the S by the Drau or Drave, by which it is separated from Slavonia; and on the W by the circle of Sumeg. It lies between 45° 33' and 46° 20' N lat., and 17° 40' and 19° E long., and contains a superficial area of 1,920 sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 266,620, of whom the great majority are Hungarians and Catholics; about 30,000 belong to the Reformed church, and 14,000 to the orthodox Greek church. The surface is highly diversified; on the SE, adjacent to the Danube and the Drave, it is flat and marshy; on the N it is intersected by ramifications of the Styrian mountains of secondary and tertiary formation. Besides the two great bounding rivers, it is watered by the Karasitza and the Oravitza. The soil is fertile, and produces wheat, lint, tobacco, and red and white wines. Considerable tracts are covered with forests, chiefly of oak, in which immense herds of swine are pastured. The mineral productions are limestone, porphyry, slate, alum, and coal. The latter mineral, which is rare in Hungary, is wrought near Vasas. It is divided into 6 circles, viz., Funfkirchen, St. Lorintz, Siklos, Baranyovar, Mohats, and Metvek, and contains one royal town, Funfkirchen or Pecs, 11 market-towns, and 341 villages.

BARANYOVAR, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Baranya, upon a small tributary of the Danube, 25 m. SE of Funfkirchen. There are some remains of an ancient castle here which gave name to the comitat.

BARAONG, a central district in the prov. of Shahabad, in Hindostan, on the l. bank of the Son, or Sone. Area 356 sq. m. Pop. 144,490. The W parts of this district are the richest in soil, and most BAR populous. The cap., of the same name, is a poor village about 15 m. W of the Son.
BARAS-KHOTUN, a large ruined town of Mongolia, in N lat. 48°, on the banks of the Kherlon, supposed to have been built by the descendants of Jenghiz Khan in the 14th cent.

BARATARIA, a lake of Louisiana, U. S., in Jefferson co., in the delta of the Mississippi, SSW of New Orleans. It is 22 m. long, and 6 m. wide; and discharges its waters into the head of the gulf of the same name. It is sometimes known as LAKE QUACHA. -Also an island at the entrance of the gulf of Barataria, in N lat. 29° 5'.

BARATARIA BAY, a deep indentation on the coast of Louisiana, U.S., near the mouths of the Mississippi, surrounded by a flat swampy country. It extends 18 m. in length from N to S, between Plaquamines co. on the E, and Jefferson and Interior counties on the W. Lat. 29° 20' N, long. 90 W. It communicates by a creek with the lake of the same name, and by means of it with the Mississippi; and thus affords a navigable passage for boats, from New Orleans to the sea.
BARAU, or Bayarow, a town of Austria, in the circle of Prachin, in Bohemia, upon the Blenitz, a tributary of the Moldau, 50 m. NW of Budweiss. Pop. 1,315.
BARAWICH, a district of the Sahara, between Tuat and Timbuctu.
BARBACENA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 12 m. NW of Elvas. Pop. 600.

BARBACOAS, a town of Ecuador, on a rivulet to which it gives its name, and near its confluence with the Patia, 130 m. N by W of Quito.—Also a town of Ecuador, in the dep. of Truxillo, to the SE of Lake Para.

BARBA'DOS, or BARBADOES, the most eastern of the Caribbean islands, and the oldest colony of Britain, situated at the SE extremity of the great American archipelago, 70 m. to the E of St. Vincent. It lies NW and SE; and is of an oval form, about 21 m. in length, and 14 m. in breadth [Maycock], with an area of 166 sq. m., or 106,470 acres, being somewhat less than that of the isle of Wight. Surface and Geology.] B. may be seen from sea at 12 leagues' distance, yet its surface is comparatively level, except in the NE quarter. On the N, S, and W sides it rises in a series of four or five horizontal strata or terraces, which in some parts exhibit mural and rugged faces. On the E it rises abruptly from the shore to a height of 50 or 70 ft. Mount Hillaby, the highest point, has an alt. of 1,147 ft. above sea-level; and from this eminence, one range of calcareous heights runs S and E to the SE point of the island; while another runs N and E to its NE point. The absolutely flat lands or plains occupy chiefly the N and S parts of the island; a narrow belt of low land borders the W coast; and a low tract of land runs from Bridgeton to the E coast of the p. of Saint Philips, which, if the sea were to rise 50 or 60 ft. above its present level, would form a narrow strait, and divide B. into two islets of an equal size. The N, S, and W sections consist of rocks formed of an aggregate of shells and madrepore rocks mixed with different kinds of coral, partly consolidated into a mass by the attrition of the water, and partly porous and full of cavities formed by the disintegration of the shells and madrepores. To the E of this, in the district from its hilly and bleakish character called Scotland, it is composed of strata of slate alternating with limestone, and an aggregate cemented with lime, and having every appearance of being the transition rock on which the madrepores and corals were found. [Maclure.] Petroleum exudes in many places from the clay hills; and saline springs make their appearance at a considerable height above sealevel; yet there are no appearances of volcanic action. A coral reef runs off all the E and N side at about 2 m. from the shore; and in making the island from the E, it is advisable to get into the lat. of 12° 56' N, and then steer for the S end. Climate.] B. is considered to be peculiarly healthy though warm, the heat being tempered by the NE trade-wind which constantly blows over the island, beginning generally about 10 A. M., and continuing till sunset: moreover, being nearly cultivated throughout, injurious miasmata are unknown. In 1844, the range of the therm., and fall of rain, were as follows: Mean at 9 A. M. Mean at 3 P. M. Rain gauge. 3.10 inches. December, The mean of these observations gives a temp. of 79° 49' at 9 A. M., and of 81° 72' at 3 P. M.; and the total fall of rain 71.96 inches. The prevailing uniformity of temp., which the above table indicates, may be considered as "one of the chief sources of the salubrity of the island. It is not the absolute degree of temp. which determines the healthfulness of a country, but the presence or absence of sudden changes of heat and cold. Tubercular consumption is almost unknown; the distressing intermitting fevers which prevail along the coast-regions in Demerara, and in some of the adjacent islands, and which sometimes baffle the physician's skill, are not met with among the natives of B.: on the contrary, those who suffer from such causes are advised to resort to B. for the restoration of their health." [History of Barbadoes. - In the awful hurricanes, however, with which it has from time to time been visited, this island labours under a formidable disadvantage. By one of these, in 1780, which lasted for 48 hours, 3,000 human beings perished, and property was destroyed to the amount of upwards of £1,000,000. In the great storm of August 11th, 1831, B. suffered quite as dreadfully as any other of the West Indian islands. Upwards of 2,500 persons were killed; and the loss of property was estimated at £2,500,000. Soil and productions.] The soil of B. generally is thin and superficial, but varies much. In the lower lands it is prevailingly a rich black mould, peculiarly suited to the sugar-cane, but now requiring liberal manuring. Here and there a red clay of considerable depth occurs: and on the hills we find a light, whitish, chalky earth, broken into a grey mould, or hardened into indurated lumps.—The staple productions of this island are sugar, rum, molasses, arrowroot, aloes, and cotton. Coffee, cocoa, tamarinds, ginger, indigo, tobacco, fustic, and logwood, are also cultivated, but not extensively. There are about 500 sugar-estates in the island; and the amount of property annually created is estimated at £2,500,000. Exports and Imports. In 1786, the principal exports were 8,659 hhds. of sugar, 114 hhds. of molasses, 5,199 hhds. of rum, 8,070 bags of ginger, and 8,864 bags of cotton. In 1814 the quantity of sugar imported into England from B. was 214,492 cwts.; in 1831, 379,052 cwts.; in 1836, 373,428 cwts.; in 1841, 257,108 cwts.; and in 1846, 302,498 cwts. As a sugar-producing island B. ranked next to Jamaica in 1831, and 1836; but in 1841 and in 1846 it was surpassed by Trinidad. The importation of rum from B. has been always liable to great fluctuation. Thus in 1815, it was 8,513 gall.; in 1821, only 742 gall.; in 1831, 26,733 gall.; in 1836, 738 gall.; in 1841, 250 gall.; and in 1846, only 197 gall.—B. does not at present grow either coffee or cocoa; but Bridgeton being a free port, a considerable quantity of both these articles is shipped from thence. The import of coffee into England from B. in 1831 was 2,420 lbs.; but in 1832 rose to 158,193 lbs; in 1836, it was 33,523 lbs.; in 1841, 1,513 lbs.; in 1843, 12,837 lbs.; and in 1846, only 864 lbs.—The export of molasses in 1831 was 15,562 ewts.; in 1836, 47,558 cwts.; in 1841, 27,475 cwts.; and in 1846, 73,462.—The export of aloes in 1844 was 121,446 lbs., valued at £7,084; of arrow-root, 74,320 lbs., valued at £1,103; of tamarinds, 63,055 lbs., valued presented towards the close of the seventeenth century, has much decreased and its importance declined. But the truth is, that B. presents upon an average of five years (from 1841 to 1845) a larger amount of shipping, of imports and exports, and revenue, than it did during the much-vaunted times when it was considered to have reached the zenith of its prosperity. The author has already dwelt upon the exaggerated accounts of its population given by Ligon and Oldmixon: if we, therefore, discard these accounts from our minds, and adopt the official reports to the lords commissioners of trade, we shall find that the colony has steadily advanced. It cannot be denied that a great diminution has taken place in the number of white inhabitants, who formerly composed a considerable part of the population; but it remains to be proved that this is any serious loss in a political point of view, as long as they are replaced by a sober and well-conducted population of the coloured race. The majority of the whites consisted of a class sui generis: too proud to earn their livelihood by manual labour, they were too poor to carry on the cultivation of the staple articles on their own account; they became 'squatters' upon the land, which might otherwise have been used for the production of sugar. The official report of Sir Richard Dutton affords us the means of judging of the importance of B. in 1683-84. There were 358 sugar-works in operation, and the quantity of useful land was estimated at 89,306 acres. The shipping which visited the island in the course of a year amounted to 338 vessels, of 25,774 tons aggregate burthen. The imports and exports are not mentioned, but the shipping gives us data by which to form an estimate of them. Let us now compare this account with the present agricultural, commercial, and financial resources of B. The number of sugar-estates in 1846 amounted to 491, with 506 windmills and one steam-engine; the produce of sugar and molasses amounted upon an average of five years (1841 to 1845) to 21,051 hogsheads, 1,500 tierces, and 930 barrels of sugar, and 4,720 puncheons of molasses; the number of ships which entered the port of Bridgetown upon an average during that period were 835 of 88,917 tons, manned by 6,413 seamen. The value of the exports upon a similar average amounted to £685,630 6s. sterling, the imports upon an average of three years to £624,636 10s. sterling; the revenue upon an average of five years to £76,852 9s. sterling, and the expenditure to £62,376 15s. The author of the 'Short History of Barbados,' 22,320 hogsheads of sugar (of 12 to 16 hundredweight), 14,430 hogsheads of rum, 102 hogsheads of molasses, 4,670 packages of ginger, and 600 bales of cotton. I have no specified return upon a ten years' average for the other staple articles, but the quantity of sugar exported upon an average from 1836 to 1845 amounts to 24,480 heavy hogsheads. Oldmixon, quoting from Tryon, says, that during its greatest prosperity the island exported 30,000 hogsheads of sugar, and the trade employed at that time 400 ships of 60,000 tons burthen, manned by about 2,000 seamen. The hogsheads of sugar were of only 12 hundred-weight at that time, which would give 36,000,000 pounds of sugar; 24,480 hogsheads merely at 16 hundred-weight give more than 39,000,000 pounds of sugar as an annual average produce for the last ten years, and we know that a great number of these hogsheads weigh as much as 1,800 to 1,900 pounds. It is evident from these remarks that the amount of produce has not diminished, but it is another point whether the plantations prove as remunerative as they were formerly, and it is, unfortunately, but too evident that this is not the case; the agriculturist, indeed, can only, with great exertion and all possible economy, earn a moderate return from the capital he employs in the cultivation of the soil." The principal imports into the island are provisions, especially cured fish and salted beef, flour, wines, beer, cattle, cutlery, coals, lumber, cotton and woollen goods, glass, leather, guano, and shingles. In 1839 the value of imports into B. was £783,775; in 1842, £510,116; in 1844, £594,484; the collective imports of the West India colonies in the latter year being £4,204,136. The import of beef and pork in the latter year amounted to £31,180; of beer and ale 115,185 gallons valued at £7,086; of corn, meal, and flour, £104,643. Nearly the whole amount of these items were from foreign parts. The chief British import was 3,606,830 yards of cotton goods valued at £62,198; and 912,890 yards of linen goods valued at £23,882. The value of the woollen goods was £11,845. The imports in 1846 amounted to £678,751. Ports, Roads, &c.] The only good anchorage being in Carlisle bay off Bridgeton, merchant-vessels here load and discharge their cargoes; and the sugar is sent round to this spot chiefly in small droghers of about 50 tons. A project is before the public for the introduction of railroad communication into this island.—The number of ships touching at B. annually is about 800, of an aggregate burden of 80,000 tons, and employing 5,000 men. * Population.] In 1674 the pop. of B. was estimated at 100,000 coloured or negroes, and 50,000 whites. In 1811, it amounted to 16,289 whites, 3,392 free coloured, and 62,258 blacks. In 1823 it was estimated at 100,000, of whom 73,345 were slaves. In 1834 it was returned at 102,912, of whom 82,807 were apprenticed labourers; 14,959 whites; and 5,146 free coloured. A census taken on 3d June, 1844, gives the following results:- Of whom employed in agriculture, and above 18 years of age. Parishes. Population. Females. Males. This pop. may be divided into four classes: Creole or Native Whites, European Whites, Creoles of mixed blood, and Native Blacks. "In consequence of the large white pop. in B., there exists a class of people," says Mr. H. N. Coleridge, "which I did not meet with in any other of the islands. By the laws of the colony, every estate is obliged to maintain a certain number of Whites in proportion to its extent. These men are called 'the Tenantry,' and have an indefeasible interest for their lives in a house and garden upon the respective plantations. They owe no fealty to the landlord, make him no acknowledgment, and entertain no kind of gratitude towards him. The militia is principally composed of these persons; and, with the exception of that service, the greatest part of them live in a state of complete idleness, and are usually ignorant and debauched to the last degree." [Six Months in the W. I.] The pop., already comparatively dense, is fast increasing, and emigration to the neighbouring islands seems beginning. Government.] B., in common with all the other W. I. islands acquired by settlement, is governed by a governor, a council, and an elective assembly. The recent arrangement of placing the Windward and the Leeward island groups respectively under the governors of B. and of Antigua, as governors-general, who correspond directly with the colonial office, and allotting to the other islands lieutenant-governors who communicate with their governor-in-chief as well as with the home-government, has elevated B. to the highest rank in the Windward islands. The salary of the governor and commander-in-chief of B.—who is also governor-general of Grenada, St. Vincent, Tobago, Trinidad, and St. Lucia-is £6,200, of which sum £2,666 is paid by the colony. The chief-justice of B, has a salary of £2,000; and the attorney-general £500.—The legislative council consists of 8 members; and, if there be less than 7 resident on the island, the governor may fill up their number to 12 pro tem. for the despatch of business. The council are appointed by the Crown. They hold their seats for life; and stand in the same rank in the colonies as the peerage in England, but do not possess the same independence, as they may be removed at the will of the governor, whose privy council they form. The powers of a chancellor are exercised by the governor and his council; but as in so small a community it is next to impossible that any cause should come into court in which some of these judges will not be directly or indirectly interested, they stand, Mr. Coleridge justly observes, in a situation which, according to the spirit of the British constitution, incapacitates them from exercising any judicial authority.-The assembly is composed of the representatives of the people, in 2 deputies from each of the 11 parishes, and 2 from the city of Bridgeton, chosen annually. The qualifications of an elector and a representative are the same, namely, the nominal possession of 10 acres of land, or a house of the yearly value of £10. The number of electors is about 1,100.—The Judica. ture consists of five courts of common-pleas, to each of which there are a judge and 4 assistants. From these courts, appeals lie in all causes to the governor and council. A general sessions of the peace is held ann.; and 11 police magistrates in the rural districts, guards, and amounting in all to about 4,500. As the head-quarters of the Windward islands, its garrison in time of war is seldom under 4,000 British infantry and artillery; but the peace-establishment of these troops does not exceed 1,600. The ratio of deaths among the white troops for 20 years, was 58.5 per 1,000; among the black troops only 46.—The revenue in 1821 amounted to £13,127; the expenditure to £19,271; in 1836, the revenue was £42,336; the expenditure £29,373.—The taxation is about 8s. 4d. per head.—There is no paper currency. The coins are principally Spanish; the weights and measures, English. Ecclesiastical affairs.] This island and the Leeward islands were erected in 1824 into a bishopric. The diocesan's income is £2,500 a-year; and one of his archdeacons who resides here has £500. There are 11 rectors of parishes who are paid by the houseof-assembly £500 currency per ann., or about £320 10s.; besides 14 curates, who receive £115 15s. currency per ann.—There are 29 public schools, which are supported from the colonial revenue at an annual expense of about £1,500. The Wesleyan Methodists have 3 missionaries, and 7 salaried teachers, on the island; the Moravians, 4 missionaries. Codrington college, an institution finely situated on the borders of the hilly tract called Scotland, 14 m. from Bridgeton, affords an academical education, including a course of theological instruction, to about 20 young men, chiefly candidates for holy orders. It is supported from the income of two estates left in trust by General Codrington to the Society for the propagation of Christian knowledge, about the commencement of last cent. The college expense to each commoner is about £30 sterling per ann.-Within the Windward island government there are 15 newspapers, of which 5, besides the Barbados Agricultural reporter, are published on this island. Topographical divisions.] B. is divided into 11 parishes, and 5 districts. The capital, Bridgetonformerly Bridgetown—is situated in N lat. 13° 5′, W long. 59° 36′ 24″. Pop. about 20,000.—Saint James, or Holetown, is merely a collection of a few houses, distant about 7 m. to the N; and 7 m. farther N is Speight's Town—or Spike's as it is commonly called —a considerable place with a roadsted and wharf. A few miles to windward of Bridgeton, or on the S coast, is Oustin's or Austin's, a mere hamlet. History.] B. is supposed to have been first discovered by the Portuguese, in the course of their voyages from Brazil, as early as 1536, and was then totally uninhabited. They did not make any settlement on this island; but furnished it with a breed of hogs for the benefit of such of their countrymen as might navigate the same tract. The first English who are known to have visited B., were the crew of a ship from London to Surinam in 1605. Finding it without inhabitants, they took possession of the country by fixing a cross on the spot where James tower was afterwards built, with the inscription,- "James, king of England and this island." The English did not, however, form any settlement in Barbadoes until 1624, when a few adventurers, under the patronage of the earl of Marlborough—to whom James I. had made a grant of the island— arrived upon it, and laid the foundations of the tower which still retains the name of that prince. The claims of this nobleman, however, were disputed by the earl of Carlisle, who received a general grant of all the Caribbean islands from Charles I. After some time, the latter nobleman became the sole proprietor of the island; but in 1663 the family gave up the patent, on condition of receiving a permanent and irrevocable grant of 41 per cent. on the produce of the island exported to other parts of the world, a burden which, according to the agreement, still falls upon the colonists. The planters, though they felt the disadvantage of the proprietary government, were driven into this measure by the Crown. The conduct of Clarendon, in promoting it, was afterwards thought so justly reprehensible as to form one of the articles of impeachment brought against him by the Commons in 1667. In 1669 B. was made the head-quarters of the Windward islands, which were defined to include all the islands to windward of Guadaloupe, while the other islands of the Caribbean chain were formed into a distinct command under the title of the Leeward islands. In 1692 a formidable insurrection of the Negroes took place on this island. In 1705, an ill-judged at- BAR tempt was made by the house-of-assembly to institute a papercurrency to the extent of £65,000. During the successive wars with France, and with America, the Barbadeans actively supported the British government, and remained at the same time free from foreign aggression. The most perfect tranquillity prevailed in this island on the abolition of colonial slavery in 1834; although the years 1816 and 1825 had been each marked in B. by formidable organized insurrections of the Negroes. Authorities.] Raynal's Hist. of the W. I.—Ligon's Hist. of B.— Hughes' Nat. Hist. of B .- Coleridge's Six Months in the W. I .- Maycock on the Geology of B. in Quart. Journ. of Science, vol. xl. -The Hist. of B. comprising a Geological and Statistical description of the island, &c. By Sir R. H. Schomburgk. Lond. 1848.

BARBADOS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, which rises in the Sierra Tapirauari, and flows into the Paraguay, on the r. bank, after a course of 90 m. Its sands are auriferous. - Also another stream in the same prov. by which the superfluous waters of Lakes Cervo and Rabeca are carried to the Rio Alegre, 9 m. above the junction of that river with the Guaporé.
BARBAGGIO, a commune of France, in the island of Corsica, cant. of Saint-Florent. Pop. 304.
BARBAIRA, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Capendu. Pop. 405.
BARBALHA, a village and district of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, 6 m. SE of Crato. Pop. 2,900.
BARBANÇON, a commune of Belgium, in Hainault, 20 m. S of Charleroi. Pop. 740. There are marble quarries and iron-works here.
BARBANIA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 20 m. NW of Turin.
BARBANO, a town of Austria, in the circle of Trieste, 10 m. SW of Albona, on the Arsa river.
BARBANT (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Mezieres, 10 m. WNW of Bellac. Pop. 1,273.
BARBANTANNE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Cheateau-Renard, 4 m. SW of Avignon, near the confluence of the Durance with the Rhone. Pop. 2,800.
BARBAR, a village of Algiers, in N lat. 34° 33', E long. 7° 5'. There are numerous ruins in the vicinity.
BARBARA, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Gaza, in N lat. 31° 34′, on the l. hand of the route from Gaza to Ramleh.
BARBARA. See BARBURRA.

BARBARA (SANTA), a town and district of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on a river of the same name, a tributary of the Piracicaha, 24 m. ESE of Cahete. Pop. 4,000. — There are several parishes, villages, and hamlets of this name in Brazil.—Also a town of Chili, on the island of Laxa, in the Biobio.—Also a town of Upper California, on the coast, in N lat. 34° 34', W long. 119° 17'. It has a roadsted sheltered from the NW winds, but exposed to S and SW.—Also a town of Peru, on the Apo Paro, SE of San Francisco. — Also a port on the island of Campana, off the W coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 48° 2'.—Also a village of Sardinia, 15 m. SW of Cagliari.—Also a village of Greece, in the dep. of Achaia, 28 m. W of Corinth.
BARBARA (SANTA). See ABROLHOS.
BARBARANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, 18 m. W of Padua, in the deleg. of Vicenza. Pop. 1,500.
BARBARIGO, a town of Austria, on the coast of Istria, in N lat. 44° 57'.
BARBARIN, a village on the W coast of Ceylon, 8 m. S of Caltura.
BARBARUSSI, a town of Turkey, in Wallachia, on the Danube, SW of Braila.

BA'RBARY, the name usually given to that portion of Africa which stretches along the S coast of the Mediterranean sea, from the W frontier of Egypt to the Atlantic ocean; and is bounded on the S by the ill-defined limits of the Sahara or Great Desert. It lies between the parallels of 28° and 36° N lat.; and forms the most northern general division of Northern Africa, including within its limits the empire of Marocco, the regencies of Tunis and Tripoli, and the territory of Algiers. Its utmost extent from E to W is nearly 2,600 m. Its breadth, in a direct line from N to S is very unequal; and has been variously estimated according to the proportion of the Desert which has been included under the appellation. At the widest part it cannot exceed 556 m.; at the narrowest it is not above 140 m. Name.] Various conjectures have been formed respecting the etymology of the word Barbary. Some have derived it from the general appellation Barbari, which the Romans may have applied to the inhabitants of Northern Africa. Others suppose it to have originated with its Arabian conquerors, in whose language barbar signifies 'a murmuring noise;' and who may have given this name to the country as indicative of the indistinct articulation of the native languages. Others consider it as a repetition of the word bar, 'a desert.' Bruce supposes that the term may be equivalent to Barbaria, or Berberia, 'the Country of the Berebers,' or Berbers. By the Arabians it is called Ardh-el-Moghreb, or 'the Region of the West,' that is, in respect of Egypt and Arabia. But they apply the term Moghreb more extensively than Europeans do the name Barbary, dividing the territory so designated into four sections, viz., 1st, Beled-el-Beraber, or 'the Country of the Berebers,' which they subdivide into Barka, Afrikiyah, Moghreb-el-Ausat, or 'the Middle West,' and Moghreb-el-Aksa, or 'the Farthest West,' that is Marocco; 2d, Beled-el-Jerid, or 'the Date country;' 3d, Es Sahara, or 'the Desert;' and 4th, Beled-es-Sudau, or 'the Country of the Blacks.' Herodotus divided Northern Africa into "the inhabited land," "the wild beast country," and "the Desert." Under the term "inhabited land" was comprehended the northern parts of Marocco, Algiers, Tunis, and Tripoli; "the wild beast country" nearly corresponded with the Beled-el-Jerid, or 'Land of Dates' of the Arabs, the Getulia of the later Greek and Roman authors; and "the desert" corresponded with the Sahara. Physical features.] The Sahara, and the Atlas mountain-system, the great physical features of this region, are specially described under their respective heads in our pages. The reader is also referred to the several articles Algiers, Barca, Marocco, SAHARA, TUNIS, TRIPOLI, &c. But we shall here endeavour to collect and arrange in one view such observations as apply generally to Barbary and its inhabitants. Climate.] The climate of B. is, in general, peculiarly temperate and salubrious, equally removed from the opposite extremes of drought and moisture, of heat and cold. In the coldest seasons, the therm. seldom sinks more than to the 5th degree above the freezing-point; and, in the warmest weather, it seldom rises to sultry, except when the winds blow from the Sahara. During a long residence in Marocco, M. Chenier never observed it lower than 21° above the freezing-point; and during the space of 12 years' residence at Algiers, Dr. Shaw found it so low as freezing only on two occasions when the ground was covered with snow. In the higher regions of the Atlas, however, severe cold is frequently experienced. According to the observations of the last-mentioned author, all the variations of the atmosphere, as indicated by the barometer, are comprehended within the compass of 13-10th inch, or from 29 1-10th to 30 4-10th. The air upon the coast is nearly as cool during summer as in the most temperate countries of Europe; and, even in the more inland places, the heat is greatly moderated by refreshing breezes from the snowy summits of the Atlas mountains. In the southern provinces, however, during the months of July and August, the heats are sometimes extreme; and about the beginning of September, a suffocating wind from the Sahara, called the shume or siume, blows with great violence for the space of one, two, or even three weeks. During the prevalence of this parching blast, the ground is often heated to such a degree that it is almost impossible to walk upon it; and the inhabitants are obliged to retire to subterraneous apartments, or warehouses on the ground floor, eating nothing but fruits, and frequently sprinkling their houses with vinegar to cool the air; buckets of water, also, are thrown upon the stone-walls of the bed-chambers, to render them habitable towards night. During the dry season, from March to September, it scarcely ever rains, and the atmosphere is almost completely free of clouds; even during the rainy season, from September to March, there is seldom a day that the sun is not visible at intervals. These vernal and autumnal rains are remarkably regular, and seldom violent; but the country is occasionally subject to long-continued droughts, which never fail to produce innumerable swarms of locusts,-the most destructive enemies of vegetation, and the frequent forerunners of famine. Soil and culture.] The general appearance of the country is mountainous, tolerably covered with wood, but not well-watered. In many places it is finely varied in hill and dale; and amidst the forests and higher grounds, delightful retreats are to be found, refreshed with abundant and numerous streams, filled with odoriferous plants and flowers, and yielding luxuriant and nutritious herbage. The soil, especially towards the coast and in the mountainous districts, is light and sandy, of a loose and yielding texture; but, in some of the northern provinces, it is composed of a rich black or red earth, without either clay or stone. It is capable of every kind of cultivation, and is productive in the highest degree. In the northern districts it is well-fitted for the growth of the most valuable European productions; and in the southern, it is capable of yielding every luxury peculiar to the East or West Indies. It is, in general, strongly impregnated with various I. salts; and to this circumstance, it is conjectured, may in a great measure be ascribed that extraordinary fertility for which it has always been remarkable. The culture, which it receives, is meagre and superficial in the extreme. The only manure employed is the annual burning of the long stubble, and the dung of the cattle turned out to pasture; while all the tillage bestowed is a slight scratching, about six inches deep, which is frequently performed with a wooden plough, and of which an ordinary pair of oxen is sufficient to accomplish a whole acre in one day. Two bushels and a half of wheat or barley are usually sown upon one acre; and the ordinary produce is about 12 bushels for one. A greater increase, however, is not uncommon. One grain generally puts forth ten or fifteen stalks, sometimes fifty or sixty, and in some instances even a still greater number; each of these stalks sometimes bears two ears; and these again often shoot out into a number of smaller ones, so as to afford a most extraordinary return. Horticulture in B. is, if possible, even more deficient than husbandry; the gardens are neither laid out with taste, nor kept with care; but present a confused mixture of fruit-trees, pot-herbs, and grain, neither divided by walks, nor ornamented with flowers. Yet even with this superficial cultivation, the soil yields almost every vegetable production in the greatest abundance; and were the husbandman sufficiently protected in the exercise of his labour, and the enjoyment of his gains, it is supposed to be capable of producing a hundred fold more than the consumption of its pop. requires.-The natural productions of this country are of great variety and value, well suited to the wants of the natives, and amply sufficient to supply a large exportation. The principal kinds of grain cultivated are wheat, barley, Indian corn, rice, millet, pease, beans, caravances. No oats are to be found in B.; the usual provender of the cattle is barley and cut straw. Hemp, flax, cotton, and, in some districts, tobacco, are raised in considerable quantities. Apples, pears, apricots, plums, pomegranates, cherries, dates, almonds, and all the fruits produced in the south of Portugal and Spain, are produced in this fertile country; and melons, oranges, lemons, limes, figs, grapes, strawberries, and a multitude of similar refreshing fruits, are supplied to the natives of Northern Africa, often indeed of an inferior quality, but generally in the greatest profusion. The sugarcane grows spontaneously in some places, and liquorice in amazing quantities, besides a variety of useful gums, and medicinal herbs, such as wormseed, orris-root, colocynth, &c. Everywhere along the coast are plantations of olive trees which grow to a considerable size; the argan tree, which bears a fruit resembling the walnut, yielding an excellent oil; large forests of oak and corkwood; and a peculiar tree, called arar or sandrac,—supposed to be the arbor vitæ of Theophrastus,-remarkable for its property of resisting rot and the worm. Honey and wax may also be mentioned as forming no despicable part of the produce of B. Minerals.] The prevailing mineral in this country is salt, which seems to pervade the whole soil, and of which there are innumerable pits, rocks, and springs. Saltpetre is extracted in great quantities from the earth of certain districts; and many sulphureous fountains,—some temperately warm, and others intensely hot,—are found in different parts of the country. There is very little stone of any kind fit for building; though the Numidian marble was highly celebrated by the ancients, none of it has been observed in modern times. Several crystals and spars, specimens of tale, mica, and pyrites, and a great variety of petrifactions, have been noticed by travellers. There are mines of iron, lead, copper, silver, antimony, and a mixed ore of antimony, lead, and gold, in the mountainous districts; but only the three first-mentioned metals are wrought to any great extent. Animal kingdom.] B. is richly stored with all the various classes of animated nature; and its domesticated animals have long constituted the most valuable possessions of the inhabitants. The horse, formerly the distinguishing glory of Numidia, is now greatly degenerated; but those of Western B. are still much renowned for their fleetness and activity. The cows are small, and yield little milk, seldom more than a quart at a time; but their flesh is tender and well-tasted. Sheep are reared in all parts of B.; and, owing to the abundance of aromatic herbs in the pasturage, the mutton possesses a peculiarly fine flavour. There is a species of this animal, common in the eastern districts, with immense tails containing a hard, solid fat, which is greatly esteemed by the natives, and bears a near resemblance to marrow. On the confines of the Sahara is another species, similar in shape and size to the fallow-deer; but its flesh is dry, and the wool is of a bad quality. The goats of B. form a considerable article of commerce; but are chiefly valued on account of the leather which is prepared from their skins. The camel is unquestionably the most useful animal in this quarter of the globe, on account of its well-known capacity of enduring fatigue, and at the same time requiring a small proportion of nourishment. Dromedaries are more rare than camels, and are generally brought from Guinea or Arabia. Among the wild quadrupeds of B. may be mentioned the large herds of horned cattle of the deer species, which abound in the mountainous districts; and of which one of the most remarkable is the fishtall or lerwee, as it is called by Shaw, or the aoudad, according to Jackson. It resembles a young heifer of about a year old; and has a long mane or beard depending from the lower part of its neck. It resides in the most inaccessible places; and when pursued, will throw itself headlong over lofty precipices, generally falling on its horns or shoulders, so that it is very seldom caught, and is not to be approached without great danger. The rhinoceros, whose horn is capable of receiving a high polish, and is sold in B. at an enormous price, has been enumerated among the ani- 20 mals of this region. Among other animals may be mentioned the elegant black-eyed gazelle or antelope, which is remarkably swift and timid; the horreh, a smaller kind of antelope, celebrated for the brilliant whiteness of its belly; the thaleb, or little red fox, which is very destructive to the young vines, and remarkable for its shrill and piercing cry; the sibsib, an intermediate species between the rat and squirrel, accounted by the Arabs a great delicacy; the ape, which is seen in great numbers, and often of a large size, in the Northern Atlas; the deeb or jackal, the jerboa, the porcupine, hare, rabbit, weasel, &c. Of the more ferocious wild animals, the chief are the lion, the leopard, and the panther, which are all occasionally ravenous invaders of the Arab encampments, and sometimes even infest the roads in the neighbourhood of large towns; the wild boar, whose strength is here proverbial, but which seldom attacks men, unless he be previously roused by some provocation; the hyæna or dubbah, which in B. is more stupid than fierce; and the wild cat, which is large and strong, and, when pressed with hunger, will sometimes attack the traveller with great ferocity. The bear is seldom seen, and inhabits only the upper regions of the Atlas, which are continually covered with snow. Domestic fowls, house-pigeons, and sparrows, resembling those of Europe, are remarkably plentiful in B. There is a great variety likewise of the duck species; but geese and turkeys are seldom seen. The towns, in the summer-season, are much frequented by storks, which are treated with great veneration by all Mussulmans, as emblematical of conjugal affection, and also as being very destructive to noxious reptiles. Among the wild fowl may be mentioned wood-pigeons, wild-geese, herons, bustards, flamingoes, pelicans, and plovers; partridges, which are much larger and finer feathered than those of Europe; curlews, which are found in great abundance; cuckoos, which are esteemed a great delicacy by the Arabs; the wood-cock, from the largeness of its head, called by the natives 'the ass of the partridges;' and a small cinereous-coloured bird, called haji, which lives upon insects of the beetle-kind, which it sticks upon thorns and does not eat till they begin to putrify. The crow of the Desert is a beautiful bird, somewhat larger than the common raven, with the legs and bill of a red colour. The ostrich abounds in the confines of the Sahara; those which are taken near Cape Bojador are of the largest size, and have the finest plumage of any in the world. There are also various kinds of singing-birds in this region: as the lark, the nightingale, the thrush, the starling, the blackbird, and the capsa sparrow, which is larger than the common sparrow, and coloured like the lark, and has a sweet melodious note superior to that of the canary, but which is so peculiarly delicate in its temperament, that it soon pines away under the smallest change of climate. Among the birds of prey in North Africa, the most deserving of notice are the nessir or vulture, which, next to the ostrich, is the largest bird in this quarter of the globe, and which feeds chiefly upon the horned beetle found upon the gum-ammoniac plant; and the eagle, the largest species of which has an exceedingly clear and beautiful eye of an orange colour. On the coasts of B. are found many kinds of excellent fish, particularly mullet, bream, anchovies, sardines, herring, mackerel, cod, skate, soles, plaice, turbot, and turtle. A firm and well-tasted fish called shebbel is very common in the rivers. Immense quantities of it are salted or baked, and sent into the interior of the country, where it is considered as an excellent corrective of the bad effects sometimes produced by an immoderate use of dates. Shrimps, prawns, crabs, and cray-fish are not uncommon. Oysters are found near Algiers; and mussels are both excellent and abundant. Land-tortoises are of a good quality, and of a very large size, sometimes weighing between 4 and 6 cwt.; but the inhabitants do not use them as food, and seldom catch them, except when employed by Europeans for the purpose. The insect tribes are extremely numerous, of a great variety of shapes, and remarkable brilliancy of colours. There are butterflies, libellæ, and beetles, 3 inches in length, and 4 inches between the extremities of the wings, when extended. The cicada or cricket is of a large size, and makes an incessant piercing noise during the night. Gnats, or mosquitos, are extremely numerous, especially on the banks of lakes, and are keen in their attacks upon Europeans; but the thick skins of the Arabs, exposed daily to the scorching heat of the sun, are impenetrable to their bite. But of all the insects of Barbary, the most formidable and abundant is the locust, which always comes from the south, and often continues its visits for three, five, or seven years. They partially disappear during the rainy season; and are frequently carried away by hurricanes into the sea. Scorpions, resembling a small lobster, about 3 inches in length, abound in stony places and old ruins; and often infest the cities so much that, in order to guard against their attacks, the inhabitants are accustomed to place the feet of their bedsteads in tubs or pans of water. The sting of the yellow-coloured is the most venomous; and the flesh of the animal itself, applied to the wound, is said to be the most effectual cure. Of the various kinds of serpents found in Barbary, two only are highly poisonous; namely, the buskah, of a black colour, 7 or 8 feet long, with a small head, which expands to three or four times its usual size, when about to make its attack; and the effah, or dipsas, about 2 feet in length, and of the thickness of a man's arm, beautifully spotted with yellow and brown. The boa, an enormous monster, from 20 to 80 ft. long, has been occasionally seen on the skirts of the Desert. Inhabitants.] The inhabitants of B. may be divided into three principal tribes: Moors, Arabs, and Berbers.—The Moors form the majority of the pop. in the towns and cultivated plains. Their exterior indicates them to be of mixed origin,—a melange of the ancient Mauritanian, Numidian, Phœnician, Roman, and Arabian inhabitants of this country. Their skin is more white, their features fuller, their nose less prominent, and their whole physiognomy betrays less of energy than the Arab exhibits in his entire person. They are divided into numerous tribes, bearing some resemblance to the clans of Celtic origin. Even in the appellations by which they are known, this resemblance may be traced. Ben or Beni, signifying 'son,' is prefixed to almost the whole of them, like the Mac of the Scottish Highlanders, or the O of the Irish. This division into tribes is here, as elsewhere, unfavourable to the progress of civilization. Individuals seek the honour of the clan to which they belong by every method whether justifiable or not; and their quarrels generally involve the whole tribe: in this manner, animosities are rendered perpetual, and a ferocious prejudice is deeply rooted in the mind. Within the narrow circle in which they act, the affections may frequently be strong; but they are too much confined ever to expand into that benevolence which, while it consults the universal good of humanity, certainly forms one of its most amiable features. The Moors are further distinguished into two general classes: such as inhabit the cities,—and such as dwell in tents, leading a pastoral life; but the difference between them is not much greater than that which, in other places, subsists between the inhabitants of the towns and of the country. Some have supposed the inhabitants of the cities to be Arabs, while those who inhabited the country were the true Moors; but this distinction seems to be without any real foundation. Arabic is the language spoken by all the Moors in the northern parts of Africa; but the corruptions and deviations from the purity of the original are said to be nearly in proportion to the distance of the country from Arabia. In the written language, however, the deviations are perhaps not so great as in that which is spoken; and among such as aspire at the honours of learning, Arabic is said to be spoken in much greater purity than among the vulgar. This departure from the original is no where, in the north of Africa, so great as to render the speech and writings of one district unintelligible to those of another. But the wild tribes which inhabit the mountains, speak a language altogether different from that of the Moors, and quite unintelligible to them: this has been supposed to be the original Punic or Numidian. The conjecture is not void of probability, but the proof might perhaps be difficult. The Arabs of B. are partly the descendants of the Arabian conquerors of the country, and partly emigrants from the Sahara. Like the Moors, they are divided into a number of tribes, which never intermingle by marriage, and which are almost continually at war with one another. If united among themselves, they would prove the ruling nation in B. They live in tents which have preserved the boat-like shape attributed by Sallust to the mapalia of the Numidians; and generally form their encampments at a considerable distance from any town. Their occupation is that of herdsmen; they likewise raise a little wheat and barley. The mountains and deserts of B. are inhabited by a race, which has undoubtedly a different origin from that of the Moorish or Arabian tribes, since their language is different. Of these tribes, which are denominated Barbars, Brebers or Berbers, a special account will be found in this work under the head Berbers. In B. they present rather a warlike character. Accustomed from their infancy to dangers and hardships of every kind, they acquire a vigour, agility and courage, unknown to their more effeminate neighbours. They are only nominally subject to any existing government; for, when offended, they retire to their mountains, whither it would be dangerous to follow them: and their adherence to the Mahommedan faith is of a kin to their submission to Mahommedan government. They are divided into several tribes, each governed by a sheikh; and form at present four distinct nations: 1st, The Amazigh, called by the Moors Shilla, or Shulla, in the mountains of Marocco; 2d, the Cabyles, in the mountains of Algiers and Tunis; 3d, the Tibbus; and 4th, the Tuaricks. Besides the tribes already mentioned, this region is inhabited by Negroes, who are purchased as slaves but treated with much humanity; and Jews, who are said to possess the greater part of the knowledge, and almost the whole trade of the country. The intolerance and oppression which this singular people suffered in Spain and Portugal drove vast multitudes of them to seek shelter among the barbarians of Africa. It has been loosely stated that 100,000 took refuge in Marocco, and about half that number in the other Barbary states. Manufactures.] The manufactures of B. are chiefly such as are requisite for the supply of the inhabitants; and are seldom prepared for exportation. The principal articles produced in the country, are, the burnoose and haic of white wool and cotton, or cotton and silk, made almost entirely by the women, and woven with their fingers without the aid of a shuttle; silk handkerchiefs, which are manufactured chiefly in the city of Fez; various kinds of silk stuffs, frequently chequered with cotton; red caps, most of which are made at Tetuan; a coarse linen stuff, of which the best is produced in Susa; carpeting, nearly equal to that of Turkey; beautiful matting made of the leaves of the palmette, or wild palm-tree; paper of a very inferior quality; muskets and sabres of Biscay iron, well-tempered by means of certain waters in the country well adapted for that purpose; gunpowder of a very glutinous nature, exceedingly apt to imbibe humidity, and deficient in strength and inflammability; leather, made of goat skin, the mode of tanning which they are very careful to conceal, and the softest and finest of which, resembling silk and impervious to water, is prepared at Tafilet.
