The manufactures of Algiers and Tunis are brought to a state of greater perfection than those of Marocco; and the inhabitants of the northern districts are a more enterprising and commercial people, than those of the south. The Moors are utterly unacquainted with the art of casting cannon, the manufacture of glass, the invention of pumps, and the use of wheel-carriages. Trade.] The Moors take no care to make or repair public roads, and have few bridges: hence their inland traffic is extremely limited, and is confined almost entirely to the markets or fairs which are held in different districts for the accommodation of the neighbouring inhabitants. At these fairs they assemble from a considerable distance to buy and sell cattle, corn, vegetables, dried fruits, carpets, haics, and the various productions of their country. All the states of Barbary, indeed, by means of caravans, carry on a lucrative and extensive commerce with Mecca, the most consecrated seat of their religious faith; and with Timbuctu, the great emporium of central Africa. For the expedition to Mecca, several thousands of camels, horses, and mules are collected, carrying merchandise to the value of two millions of dollars. Besides woollen stuffs, leather, indigo, cochineal, ostrich feathers, the traders never fail to take with them, or to purchase by the way, such articles of commerce as can be sold with profit, at Alexandria, Cairo, and the other towns through which they pass. These companies of merchants and pilgrims-for the two characters are generally united in this journey to Mecca-bring back with them Levant and Persian silks and muslins, amber, musk, essence of roses, &c. The caravans, which penetrate the interior of Africa, are neither so numerous nor so valuable as those which go to Mecca. They travel through the desert of Sahara, occupying several months in this toilsome journey. The articles which they transport to Timbuctu, are linens, muslins, silks, light haics, red caps, spices, sugar and tea, but chiefly tobacco and salt; and the produce, returned from Sudan, consists principally of bars of gold, gold dust, and gold trinkets-in the manufacture of which the natives of that country display great ingenuity-elephants' teeth, gums, and slaves, besides ambergris and ostrich-feathers, collected by the way on the confines of the Desert. We copy from the columns of the Constitutionnel the following interesting article on the subject of the internal commerce of Africa:-"The fact is now placed beyond a doubt that a very considerable commercial movement formerly existed between the B. regencies, Marocco, and the interior of the African continent. The want of security may, perhaps, have retarded it for some years, but the tendency, the necessities, and the habits of the people still remain, and it will be possible, by well-organized efforts, to restore to this commerce all its importance. The routes by which the goods and merchandise disembarked at Bona, Algiers, or Oran, are conveyed, are at present perfectly well-known: upon these routes are observed towns which serve as magazines or storehouses for the caravans, or as entrepots for the commodities; such seem to advance abreast towards the Desert. Thus Constantine, Medeah, Mascara, and Tlemcen form, on departing from the sea-shore, the first line of markets for the produce of Europe. Tibessa, Biscara, Bouçaada, Laghouat, and Stitten, constitute the second line; and on the third, we find the Oued-Souf, Tuggurt, Ouerguela, and Mettili. Further on, but still presenting themselves in a parallel line, are El-Sedames, El-Colea, and El-Arib. The merchandise which from these three last points is sent forward towards the south, meets on its route with a most important market, that divides the crossing of the Desert, and forms the great oasis of Tuat. From this station the caravans, which are there assembled by concentration, take different routes in order to proceed to the various parts of the Sudan. All commerce in that country is carried on by means of caravans. Exposed to the attacks and the robberies of the inhabitants of the Desert, the merchants are compelled to purchase the protection of the chiefs of these pirates of the Sahara. Amongst these tribes, the greatest and most numerous is, without doubt, that of the Touareg [Tuaricks]. These nomades are in the habit, when they cross the Desert, of placing inscriptions upon all the stones with which they meet. But what is still more curious, and worthy of riveting the attention of all our archæologists and philologists, is the fact that these inscriptions are written in characters which do not resemble in the slightest degree the Arabic characters, and which the natives look upon as the written alphabet of the Targuia, or Barbary, tongue. The most credible conjectures give reason to believe that these characters are borrowed from the Phœnician writings. At Ouerguela there are artesian wells hollowed out of the stone, and the piercing of which is ascribed by the tradition of the country to Alexander the Great. From Tuggurt to Timbuctu, the caravans are 37 days on the road, including 29 encampments; but eight days are devoted to repose at different parts of this long march." The commercial intercourse between the N of Africa and the kingdoms of Europe is extremely limited and fluctuating; the treaties, which have been formed at different periods between the trading nations of Christendom and the piratical states of Barbary, were intended rather for securing protection from the African corsairs, than for promoting a mutual exchange of commodities. The instability and tyranny of the several native governments in B. must still be regarded, as an almost insuperable obstacle to the industry of the natives, and to the confidence of foreigners. The wants of the inhabitants also are few and simple; and those habits of luxury, which are the great springs of BAR commerce, are either altogether unknown among them, or at least, restrained from open indulgence, by the lawless acts of extortion to which every opulent individual is invariably exposed. The little foreign traffic, which the people, or rather the rulers, of B. do carry on, has been chiefly engrossed, of late years, by the French, Americans, and British. The principal articles imported into the N of Africa, are, broad-cloths, linens, muslins, silks, metals, hardware, mirrors, sugar, tea, gunpowder, and dollars; and those hitherto exported are, gums, almonds, dates, aromatic seeds, ivory, leather, hides, ostrich feathers, olive-oil, wax, and wool. History.] Barbary is one of the earliest known regions of Africa. The Phoenicians navigated along its coasts above 1,000 years before our era; and a colony of this people founded here the celebrated city of Carthage in 886 B. C. After the destruction of that city, the Romans disputed the possession of B. with the Moors, Getulians, Numidians, Africans, Libyans, Cyrenaens, and Marmarides; and having at last obtained the undisputed sovereignty of the country, they divided it into the five provinces of Pentapolis, Cyrenaica, Africa Propria, Numidia, and Mauritania. In the time of Constantine, that part of the country which lies to the E of the Greater Syrtis, or gulf of Sidra, was a dependence of the province of Egypt; that which lay to the W of the Mullavia appertained to the province of Spain; and the country between these two regions formed a distinct province, known by the name of Africa or Afrikiyah. In 428 the Vandals passed into Africa, and rendered themselves masters of all that the Romans once possessed in this quarter of the globe. The career of these fierce conquerors was marked by devastation and blood; but, about the year 530, Belisarius recovered B. for the Greek emperor, and it formed a part of the Eastern empire until torn from it in 697 by the resistless arms of the Mahommedan Arabs. Under its first Saracen princes, Northern Africa flourished in sciences and art, and almost regained its pristine grandeur. A period of gradual decline succeeded. The Saracens, after having planted their standard in the most valuable provinces of Spain, at last found themselves unable to maintain a footing even in Africa itself. They called in the Turks to their aid; and these latter conquerors laid the foundation of those maritime states of Barbary which so long harassed the Christian powers of Europe by their piratical depredations. This extensive and beautiful region, separated from Europe only by a sea of no great width, has often been the centre of an advanced civilization. It was distinguished for prosperity, population, and industry, under the Carthaginians, Romans, Vandals, and Arabians. Its commercial advantages are very great. Its intercourse with the coasts of Europe is far more easy and quick than that of those coasts with their own capital cities, and the transportation of goods is less expensive from Marseilles and Genoa, to Tunis and Algiers, than to Paris, or even to Turin and Milan. Cato showed to the Roman senate fresh figs, which were gathered under the walls of Carthage; yet this fruit, except in its dried state, is not edible after three days from the time of gathering. The whole country can support 60,000,000 of inhabitants, and now hardly contains 10,500,000. Next to Egypt, it was the richest and most productive Roman province, and one of the granaries of the mistress of the world. The Roman writers called it "the soul of the republic," "the jewel of the empire,"- "speciositas totius terræ florentis," and the wealthy citizens considered the possession of palaces and country-houses on this beautiful coast as the highest happiness. The little Arabian courts, too, of Fez, Tetuan, Tremecen, Garba, Constantine, sedulously encouraged the arts and agriculture. Amalfi, Naples, Messina, Pisa, Genoa, and Florence, enriched themselves by their commercial intercourse with this fine country, and the Venetian ships visited all the cities of the African coast. Three centuries ago, an end was put to all this prosperity; and the land became the abode of crime and misery, the prey of adventurers, collected together from another part of the world, and detested by the native inhabitants. Authorities.] Letters from Barbary, &c.—Abbé Poiret's Travels in Barbary.—Shaw's Travels in Barbary.—Chenier's State of Morocco.—Lempriere's Tour to Morocco.—Bruce's Travels.—Jackson's Account of Morocco.—Heeren's Historical Researches and Reflections.—Blaquiere's Letters.—Captain Beechey's Cyrenaica.
BARBAS (CAPE), a promontory of Western Africa, on the coast of the Sahara, forming the S side of the bay of St. Cyprian, in N lat. 22° 20'.
BARBASA, or Barbosa, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. of Antioquia, 20 m. NE of Medellin, at an alt. of 450 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,493.
BARBASAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of St. Bertrand, on the r. bank of the Garonne. Pop. 520. There is a thermal spring here.
BARBASTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Lavardac. It has manufactories of corks, measures, and vinegar, and possesses an active trade. Pop. 1,530.

BARBA'STRO, or BALBASTRO, a district and town of Spain, in Aragon. The district stretches on the N to the Pyrenees, and touches the frontier of Catalonia on the SE. It comprises the upper valley of the Cinca, and the valleys of Puectolas and Solana. The level portion is fertile and well-cultivated. The town of B. is in the prov. and 27 m. SE of Huesca, on the Vero, an affluent of the Cinca. It is surrounded by walls, and is capable of defence. It is the see of a bishopric, suffragan of Saragossa, and is the cap. of the partido. Pop. 6,043.

BARBATA, a river of Spain, in Andalusia, which takes its rise in the Sierra Grillemena, on the NE confines of the prov. of Granada, runs SSW, and then, curving towards the W, unites with the Guadix. Thence, under the name of the Guadiana Menor, it takes a N direction, and, after a course of 60 m., falls into the Guadalquiver at the town of the same name, and 5 m. SE of Ubeda.-Also a river of N Africa, in Algiers, which unites with the Tafna, 15 m. above the entrance of that river into the gulf of Tremezin.
BARBATRE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vendee, com. of Nourmoutiers. Pop. 1,200.
BARBAUTAN, or BARBITAN, a village of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Cazabon, 42 m. NW of Auch. Pop. 215. Its hot baths render it a place of considerable resort in summer.

BARBAXEDA, a mountain of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 39 m. N of Cuenca, near the town of Boteta. It contains coal at a considerable elevation upon it; near the village of Gobar are two extensive lakes abounding with fish.

BARBAZAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Tournay, at the base of a mountain on the r. bank of the Garonne, 8 m. SW of St. Gaudens. In the vicinity of the village is a tepid mineral spring; and there are marble quarries in the environs. Pop. 269.
BARBE (St.), or PANUKY, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Chinese sea, in N lat. 0° 9', and E long. 107° 15'.

BARBENCON, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, in the cant. and 2 m. ESE of Beaumont, 20 m. SE of Mons. It consists of about 100 houses, and contains a castle. Lace, which is extensively manufactured here, and marble which is quarried in the environs, form its chief articles of commerce. It was ceded to Belgium by the treaty of 1815.

BARBERINO-DI-MUGELLO, a town of Tuscany, 15 m. N of Florence, on the l. bank of the Sieve, and at the base of the Apennines. It has extensive manufactories of straw bonnets. In the environs is the royal villa of Cafaggiolo, the ancient residence of the family of Medicis. Pop. 1,084.

BARBERINO-DI-VAL-D'ESLA, a town of Tuscany, 15 m. SSW of Florence. It is celebrated as the birth-place and ancestral residence of the family of Pope Urban VIII. The environs afford an excellent species of cheese. Pop. 725.

BARBEZIEUX, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Charente.—The arrond. has a superficies of 102,439 hectares, with a pop. of 55,532 in 1836; and of 56,077 in 1846; and comprises the 6 cant. of Aubeterre, Baignes, Barbezieux, Brossac, Chalais, and Montmoreau.—The cant. comprises 21 com., and had a pop. in 1831 of 14,063.—The commune and town are 20 m. SW of Angouleme, in N lat. 45° 28' 24", W long. 0° 9′ 28″. The town is built on the ascent of a hill at the foot of which flows the Saugne, an affluent of the Charente. The principal manufacture of the place is that of coarse towelling. The commerce consists in the agricultural produce of the surrounding district, and in truffles. Pop. in 1836, 3,013; in 1846, 3,303.—In the middle ages this town was called Barbesie or Barbesillum, and possessed a castle of great strength, of which some re- BAR 580 mains still exist in the prison.—There is a mineral spring in the neighbourhood.
BARBEZON, a small stream of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Figeac. It rises near La Trouquiere, and flows S to the Selle, which it joins 3 m. NE of Figeac, after a course of 12 m.
BARBIANELLO, a village of Sardinia, in the prov. and 12 m. NE of Voghera.
BARBICE. See BERBICE.

BARBILLA, a stream of Central America, in the dep. of Costa Rica, which unites with the Chirripó at Real, and forms the Matina. It is navigable before its confluence with the Chirripó, and in the rainy season brings down a large volume of water.

BARBO, or PATOOK, a river of Central America, in the prov. of Honduras, which flows from SSW to NNE, into the Caribbean sea, near Patook point, in N lat. 15° 49', E long. 84° 18'. It has a total length of course of 130 m., with a mean breadth of half a cable's length. It is connected with Beaver's lagune by an inferior arm.
BARBOLLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NE of Segovia, in the partido of Sepulveda, near the r. bank of the Cuevas.
